agent_outputs.raw: The mortality comparison cannot support the conclusion that the model improved care. Three confounds. First, comparing November-December to July-September crosses seasons with different emergency department case mix — winter respiratory and cardiovascular cases differ structurally from summer presentations. Second, the hospital simultaneously updated antibiotic guidance and opened a winter surge unit. The antibiotic protocol change alone is a well-established mortality-reducing intervention in sepsis; it cannot be dismissed as background noise. Third, the analysis was restricted to patients with early lactate testing, and if clinicians changed their lactate ordering practices after the model went live or after the protocol update, the included patient population changed between comparison periods. Without a concurrent control, none of these confounds can be separated. Critique wins.

Three structural problems with the mortality analysis. One: before-after comparison across seasons cannot be attributed to the model without controlling for case-mix differences — mortality rates in emergency departments vary significantly between summer and winter months. Two: two major concurrent operational changes (antibiotic protocol, surge unit) provide alternative explanations for mortality improvement. The antibiotic protocol is especially important — timing and selection of antibiotics in sepsis is a critical mortality determinant. Three: inclusion based on lactate ordering creates a population that depends on clinician behavior, which may have changed with the model or protocol. Critique wins.

The mortality improvement observed after model deployment cannot be attributed to the model because the analysis has three confounds that the study design does not control for: seasonal case-mix differences, concurrent care protocol changes, and selection bias through the lactate ordering criterion. The model's internal validation (prediction accuracy) does not establish downstream outcome impact. A concurrent control group using a stepped-wedge design or comparison to prior-year same-season outcomes with the same antibiotic protocol is required. Critique wins.

synthesis: Unanimous critique_wins. All three agents identify the same three confounds. The concurrent antibiotic protocol change is consistently identified as a particularly strong alternative explanation for the mortality improvement.

etd_constraint.condition: Concurrent control comparison: EDs with new antibiotic protocol but without model alert in same season; or prior-year same-season comparison with available protocol context
etd_constraint.supports_critique_if: Control EDs with antibiotic protocol but without model show comparable mortality improvement; lactate ordering rates differ materially between comparison periods
etd_constraint.supports_defense_if: Model-active EDs show lower mortality than concurrent control EDs with identical antibiotic protocol in the same season
etd_constraint.ambiguous_if: Outcomes improve in model EDs but partial improvement also seen in control EDs making model contribution unclear

all_issues_raised: Concurrent antibiotic protocol and surge unit (all three agents)
Lactate ordering selection bias (all three agents)
